refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation link availability ≥ 0.999 (99.9%) over the agreed evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the bidirectional command-and-telemetry link is operational to total evaluation time.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-010 addresses a critical operational requirement: remote equipment control depends on reliable communication. The refined FR quantifies the availability target, specifies bidirectionality (command uplink + telemetry downlink), and anchors measurement to an agreed evaluation period. This is essential for safety-critical teleoperation where link loss can lead to loss of control.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Continuous monitoring of link state (up/down) during evaluation period; log all link transitions; calculate availability = (total_time - downtime) / total_time; compare against 0.999 threshold. Acceptance requires independent verification by Safety Manager and Site Manager.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional command uplink from tele-operator to vehicle.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional telemetry downlink from vehicle to tele-operator.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The teleoperation system shall detect link loss and initiate safe state transition within 100 ms.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The teleoperation system shall provide real-time link quality metrics (signal strength, latency, packet loss) to the tele-operator.
FR-NGHE-010.5: The teleoperation system shall support redundant communication paths to achieve 0.999 availability.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Single communication link (cellular or RF) with no redundancy; rely on network provider SLA.
DP-B: Dual independent communication paths (e.g., cellular + satellite or dual cellular carriers) with automatic failover; active link monitoring and health-based switching.
DP-C: Mesh network with local relay nodes at worksite; reduces dependency on external carriers; adds infrastructure cost.
DP-D: Hybrid approach: primary cellular link + secondary satellite link with intelligent arbitration; includes local edge caching of critical commands.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-B: Dual independent communication paths with automatic failover and active link monitoring. Primary path uses commercial cellular (LTE/5G); secondary path uses licensed RF or satellite backup. Automatic switching triggered by link quality degradation (latency > threshold, packet loss > threshold, or explicit link-down event). Real-time link metrics streamed to tele-operator UI.

potential_sources: DP-B couples teleoperation link availability to network infrastructure selection (cellular carrier, satellite provider, RF spectrum licensing). This is acceptable because the FR explicitly requires customer-defined availability; infrastructure choice is a design trade-off.
DP-B couples link failover logic to vehicle-side receiver/transmitter hardware. This is acceptable; the coupling is localized to the Teleoperation module.
DP-B does NOT couple to vehicle propulsion, safety interlocks, or payload control; these remain independent.
coupling_check.notes: Coupling is minimal and localized to communication subsystem. No evidence of zigzag (circular dependency between FR and DP). DP-B is implementable without requiring redesign of other system domains.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Dual independent carriers (cellular + satellite or dual cellular) are available at the worksite. Risk: Remote or rural sites may have limited carrier coverage. Mitigation: Conduct site survey; include carrier availability as a pre-deployment gate.

Assumption: Failover switching can occur in < 100 ms without loss of critical commands. Risk: Switching latency may exceed 100 ms if not carefully engineered. Mitigation: Prototype and test failover timing; implement command buffering during transition.

Assumption: Link quality metrics (latency, packet loss) can be reliably measured in real time. Risk: Measurement overhead may degrade performance. Mitigation: Use lightweight probing (e.g., periodic ping) rather than deep packet inspection.

Assumption: Tele-operator can respond to link quality alerts and manually switch paths if needed. Risk: Operator may not react quickly enough. Mitigation: Implement automatic failover; manual override is secondary.

Risk: Satellite link introduces higher latency (500+ ms round-trip) compared to cellular (50–100 ms). This may degrade operator experience. Mitigation: Use satellite only as backup; document latency trade-off in operational procedures.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: DP-B is sound. Recommend adding a tertiary failover option (local RF mesh or edge compute relay) if customer budget permits, to further reduce dependency on external carriers. For now, proceed with DP-B as primary design.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns all logic and hardware for establishing, monitoring, and maintaining bidirectional command and telemetry links between tele-operator and vehicle. Includes link state machine, failover arbitration, link quality monitoring, and interface to vehicle receiver/transmitter hardware. Does NOT include vehicle propulsion control, safety interlocks, or payload logic (those are owned by Vehicle module). Does NOT include worksite safety policies or operator training (those are owned by Safety Services module).

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Tele-operator sends command messages (movement, speed, payload control) via Tasking interface. Teleoperation module receives Tasking, encodes into protocol, and transmits over primary or secondary link.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle sends sensor data (position, orientation, payload state, link quality metrics) via Telemetry interface. Teleoperation module receives Telemetry from vehicle, decodes, and forwards to tele-operator UI and Safety Services.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module receives SafetyAlert from Safety Services (e.g., 'link loss detected, initiate safe state'). Teleoperation module sends SafetyAlert to Safety Services if link quality degrades below threshold or failover occurs.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module interfaces with Network Link (cellular, satellite, RF hardware) to transmit commands and receive telemetry. Includes link state queries, failover signaling, and link quality probing.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the top-level system container. Teleoperation is a major subsystem but not the entire solution. Assigning IM to NGHE Solution would diffuse responsibility across all subsystems (Vehicle, Safety Services, etc.). Not recommended as primary IM boundary.

Vehicle module owns propulsion, payload, and onboard sensors. Teleoperation link logic (failover, link monitoring) is not inherently a vehicle function; it is a communication subsystem function. Assigning to Vehicle would couple communication concerns with vehicle dynamics. Not recommended.

Teleoperation module is explicitly designed to own remote command-and-control logic. DP-B (dual-path failover, link monitoring) maps directly to Teleoperation responsibilities. All external interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are natural boundaries for this module. Highly cohesive; minimal diffusion. RECOMMENDED.

Safety Services owns safety policies, interlocks, and alerts. Link availability is a safety concern, but the mechanism (dual-path failover) is a communication engineering concern, not a safety policy concern. Assigning IM to Safety Services would blur the boundary between safety policy and communication infrastructure. Not recommended.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the natural owner of DP-B (dual-path failover with link monitoring). The module's scope includes all command-and-telemetry link logic, and the external interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) align with DP-B's requirements. Containment score 0.95 indicates high cohesion and low diffusion.
containment_risks: Risk: Teleoperation module may become too large if it also owns link encryption, compression, and protocol versioning. Mitigation: Define clear sub-boundaries within Teleoperation (e.g., LinkManager, ProtocolHandler, FailoverArbitrator) to maintain modularity.

Risk: Network Link interface is external to Teleoperation; if Network Link hardware is not available or is delayed, Teleoperation module cannot be tested in isolation. Mitigation: Develop mock Network Link interface for unit testing; conduct integration testing with real hardware.

Risk: SafetyAlert interface couples Teleoperation to Safety Services. If Safety Services changes its alert schema, Teleoperation must adapt. Mitigation: Define SafetyAlert interface contract clearly; use versioning to manage evolution.

Risk: Failover logic may require real-time scheduling guarantees (< 100 ms switching). If Teleoperation module runs on a non-real-time OS, this may not be achievable. Mitigation: Conduct timing analysis; consider dedicated real-time kernel or hardware-based failover if needed.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Assign IM to Teleoperation module. Recommend further decomposition of Teleoperation into sub-modules (LinkManager, FailoverArbitrator, MetricsCollector) at the next level to manage complexity and ensure testability. Ensure Network Link interface is well-defined and mocked for unit testing.

fr_dp_gate.reason: Refined FR (0.999 availability, bidirectional link, measured over agreed period) is directly addressed by DP-B (dual-path failover with active monitoring). DP-B provides a clear mechanism to achieve the quantified availability target. No ambiguity or missing logic. FR and DP are well-aligned.

diffusion_risks: Teleoperation module must interface with Network Link (external hardware). If Network Link is not ready or is owned by a different team, integration risk increases. Mitigation: Establish clear interface contract and mock Network Link for early testing.

Failover logic requires coordination with Safety Services (SafetyAlert interface). If Safety Services changes its alert schema, Teleoperation must adapt. Mitigation: Version the SafetyAlert interface; establish change control process.

Dual-path failover may require real-time scheduling. If Teleoperation runs on a non-real-time OS, timing guarantees may not be met. Mitigation: Conduct timing analysis early; escalate to architecture if real-time kernel is needed.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module has high containment (0.95) for DP-B. All failover logic, link monitoring, and metric collection are naturally contained within Teleoperation. External interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined and do not diffuse responsibility. IM assignment is sound.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of FR-NGHE-010 (0.999 availability) is feasible via continuous link state monitoring during evaluation period. Teleoperation module can log all link transitions and compute availability metric. Independent verification by Safety Manager and Site Manager is required. Acceptance criteria are clear and measurable.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation of FR-NGHE-010 to Teleoperation module is clear. Sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-010.1 through 010.5) are allocated to Teleoperation and can be further decomposed at the next level. No ambiguity about ownership or responsibility.

recommended_revisions: Clarify the evaluation period for availability measurement (e.g., 30 days, 90 days, continuous). This should be defined in the customer contract and referenced in FR-NGHE-010.
Define link quality thresholds (latency, packet loss) that trigger failover. These should be tuned based on worksite conditions and operator feedback.
Establish a change control process for SafetyAlert interface to manage evolution and prevent coupling issues.
Conduct timing analysis for failover switching (target < 100 ms) early in design; escalate to architecture if real-time kernel is needed.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-010 is not atomic; it has been refined into five sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-010.1 through 010.5) that address distinct concerns: command uplink, telemetry downlink, link loss detection, link quality metrics, and redundancy. Each sub-FR requires further decomposition to define detailed DPs and IMs. DP-B (dual-path failover) is sound and well-aligned with the refined FR. Teleoperation module is the correct IM boundary with high containment (0.95). All gates pass. Proceed to decompose sub-FRs at the next level.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional command uplink from tele-operator to vehicle.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional telemetry downlink from vehicle to tele-operator.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The teleoperation system shall detect link loss and initiate safe state transition within 100 ms.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The teleoperation system shall provide real-time link quality metrics (signal strength, latency, packet loss) to the tele-operator.
FR-NGHE-010.5: The teleoperation system shall support redundant communication paths to achieve 0.999 availability.

assumptions: Dual independent carriers (cellular + satellite or dual cellular) are available at the worksite.
Failover switching can occur in < 100 ms without loss of critical commands.
Link quality metrics (latency, packet loss) can be reliably measured in real time.
Tele-operator can respond to link quality alerts and manually switch paths if needed (automatic failover is primary).
Evaluation period for availability measurement is defined in customer contract (e.g., 30 days, 90 days).
Network Link hardware (cellular, satellite, RF) is available and owned by a separate team; Teleoperation module interfaces with it via well-defined contract.
open_risks: Satellite link introduces higher latency (500+ ms round-trip) compared to cellular (50–100 ms). This may degrade operator experience. Mitigation: Use satellite only as backup; document latency trade-off in operational procedures.

Failover logic may require real-time scheduling guarantees (< 100 ms switching). If Teleoperation module runs on a non-real-time OS, this may not be achievable. Mitigation: Conduct timing analysis; consider dedicated real-time kernel or hardware-based failover if needed.

Teleoperation module must interface with Network Link (external hardware). If Network Link is not ready or is owned by a different team, integration risk increases. Mitigation: Establish clear interface contract and mock Network Link for early testing.

SafetyAlert interface couples Teleoperation to Safety Services. If Safety Services changes its alert schema, Teleoperation must adapt. Mitigation: Version the SafetyAlert interface; establish change control process.
Dual-path failover adds cost and complexity. Customer may not accept the trade-off. Mitigation: Conduct cost-benefit analysis; present alternatives (DP-A, DP-C, DP-D) to customer.
evidence_needed: Prototype of dual-path failover logic with timing measurements (target < 100 ms switching).
Link quality monitoring implementation with real-time metric collection (latency, packet loss, signal strength).
Integration test with real Network Link hardware (cellular, satellite, or RF).
Availability measurement data from evaluation period (e.g., 30-day trial at customer worksite).
Operator feedback on link quality alerts and failover behavior.
Safety analysis (FMEA, FTA) for link loss scenarios and safe state transitions.
Cost estimate for dual-path infrastructure (carriers, hardware, licensing).
Customer acceptance of DP-B and availability target (0.999).
